RESPONDENT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AMENDED NOTICE OF APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE SPECIAL MOTION TO STRIKE PURSUANT TO CCP SECTION 425.16 (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar Jan-30-2025 Off Calendar

Amended Notice Of Application For Leave To File Special Motion To Strike Pursuant To Ccp Section 425.16
Off Calendar Jan-30-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: RESPONDENT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's Application For Leave To File Special Motion To Strike Pursuant To Ccp Section 425.16.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts is tentative ruling.

Respondent's Application For Leave To File Special Motion To Strike is denied.

On November 9, 2023, Petitioner Howard Maibach filed this petition for a writ of mandate against the Regents of the University of California to enforce the California Public Records Act's requirement to produce documents. Petitioner claims that UCSF published a report that Petitioner's research raised ethical concerns and perpetuated racism. Petitioner wants to force UCSF to disclose documents withheld by the university related to the investigation underlying the report. The petition was served on November 13, 2023.

CCP Section 425.16(f) provides that: "The special motion may be filed within 60 days of the service of the complaint or, in the court's discretion, at any later time upon terms it deems proper. The motion shall be scheduled by the clerk of the court for a hearing not more than 30 days after the service of the motion unless the docket conditions of the court require a later hearing." The deadline here was January 12, 2024 absent the exercise of this court's discretion.

In considering whether to grant leave for this significant extension, the court considers UCSF's reasons for delay and the prejudice to Maibach.

The prejudice is significant: Maibach's CPRA action should be a summary proceeding resulting in a quick judicial determination. Allowing a special motion to strike could delay the action significantly in light of the potential appeal that would follow. Moreover, the parties have already invested significant resources in discovery. One benefit of the anti-SLAPP motion is that it typically precedes discovery and sometimes obviates the need for it; that benefit is lost where a party delays bringing the motion.

UCSF's justification is not strong. UCSF posits that the need to file an anti-SLAPP motion only became apparent recently in discovery when Maibach contended the UCSF report was not legitimate academic research. That contention appeared on the face of the petition (e.g. Petn. para. 11), and UCSF's answer acknowledged the availability of the anti-SLAPP motion (Ans. at 1:6-9). That UCSF would now make a different strategic choice about filing an anti-SLAPP motion does not in itself justify the late filing.

Order signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
